entry.title: Re: Integration Subscription Billing with CPQ
entry.content:
Hi Maurice,

Thank you for your question – this is indeed an important topic to clarify.

The integration guide you found refers to a legacy integration that is no longer recommended. Specifically, it only supports pricing directly in rate plans (without pricing schemes) in SAP Subscription Billing. This basic pricing approach provides a limited set of pricing features and has been deprecated. If you are setting up a new tenant, this integration will not be applicable to your scenario.

The good news – a new integration is on the short-term roadmap

A new and improved CPQ integration is currently planned for delivery in Q4 2026: The roadmap item is titled "Seamless End-to-End Quoting of Subscriptions and Solutions in Cloud ERP" and you can track its progress directly on the SAP Road Map Explorer:
🔗 View Roadmap Item

We recommend keeping an eye on this roadmap item for the latest updates on scope and delivery timelines. 

If you need further details or support with your integration project please reach out to me directly. 

Best regards,

Boris

entry.title: Re: Change of billing material determined in project billing
entry.content: You can decouple “what was recorded” (activity type) from “what is billed” (billing material) by configuring the billing material determination in the project billing profile and, if needed, enhancing it with a BAdI "PROJ_BIL_MODIFY_BDR_DATA"

entry.title: Time Recording Admissibility Differs Across Role Players
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

 

Please help me.... Employees are making use of monthly approval periods. Employees are not allowed to make changes to their timesheets retroactively however, managers are able to make changes for 1 week post the approval period (one month) with HR admins having no limits on any changes in the past.

 

How can I configure this? Time Recording Admissibility is applicable to employees only...

 

Thanks,

Manon

 SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 02 - Content for common reuse types
Re: End-to-End Forecasting with SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
Hi @appel_solar_dk , thanks for your reply! 

I agree that this sentence may sound a bit contradicting, and you are totally right about the Replication flow - this is a physical copy into the object store. 

What I was trying to say in this article though is the following. I know companies that are using BW on-prem for data warehousing and Databricks for data science tasks. To bring BW data into Databricks for further calculations, they have to physically copy data out of BW. And then physically copy the calculation result (e.g. sales forecast) back into BW. 

I compare this scenario with DSP-DBX bundle and say that data no longer needs to be copied back and forth. As I'm saying in the next sentence after the one you quoted: "once wrapped as a Data Product, BW sales data can be shared with Databricks via the Delta Share protocol". Wrapping data as a Data Product implies loading it into the object store first, this is true. 

Best, Ilya

entry.title: Re: Requirement for Reporting Purchase Order Item Revisio...
entry.content: The most reliable approach in Public Cloud is to create a custom CDS view that joins PO items with product master and explicitly exposes the revision level. If business requires it to be part of the PO item itself (not just reporting), then Custom Field & Logic is the right solution.

entry.title: S/4HANA Public Cloud EWM – Return Excess Subcontracting Components from Vendor Stock
entry.content:
Hi SAP Experts,

We are looking for guidance on the standard SAP process for returning excess subcontracting components from vendor stock in an EWM-managed environment.

Business Scenario

We create a subcontracting PO with the required component quantities.

Example:

  • Component A = 40 KG
  • Component B = 30 KG

However, due to standard pack sizes, the business does not repack materials before sending them to the subcontractor. Therefore, the quantities in the outbound delivery are increased.

Example:

  • Component A = 100 KG
  • Component B = 100 KG

The stock is successfully issued to the subcontractor using the standard subcontracting process.

Issue

After the subcontracting process is completed, a portion of the components remains unused at the subcontractor's location.

Example:

  • Component A consumed = 40 KG, leftover = 60 KG
  • Component B consumed = 30 KG, leftover = 70 KG

We need to return these leftover quantities from subcontractor special stock back to our warehouse inventory.

Questions

  1. What is the standard SAP process to return non-consumed subcontracting components from vendor stock?
  2. How is this process executed in an EWM-managed warehouse in S/4HANA Public Cloud?

Any guidance, best practices, or documentation references would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

Best Regards,

Adil Shaik.

entry.title: Re: Time Recording Admissibility Differs Across Role Players
entry.content:
Hello,
@ManonH 
Our organization operates on a monthly timesheet approval cycle. Under this policy, employees are strictly prohibited from making retroactive adjustments to their timesheets once the month has closed. However, to accommodate necessary corrections, managers are granted a one-week grace period immediately following the end of the approval period to submit modifications on behalf of their team. For any adjustments required beyond this one-week window, HR administrators retain unrestricted access to make timesheet changes at any point in the past.





Best Regards

entry.title: Modeling Dynamic Rail Transportation for Agricultural Commodities in SAP TM - Part 1
entry.content:
The Dynamic Nature of Grain Trading

Commodity traders do not operate like traditional manufacturers with stable plant-to-customer lanes. A trader may purchase grain today from a new elevator in rural Iowa and sell it tomorrow to a processor in Texas or an export terminal in the Pacific Northwest. This requires the ability to rapidly evaluate rail feasibility and costs between virtually any two points in the network.

Rail remains the backbone for bulk grain movements. Yet current SAP TM implementations face systemic barriers in supporting this fluidity.

How SAP TM Currently Models Rail Shipments?

In SAP TM, freight documents (Freight Units, Railcar Units, Freight Orders) are created between Business Partner Locations. These locations represent physical sites such as a specific grain elevator or a milling facility. Each BP Location is tied to a detailed postal address.

Default Routes — the primary mechanism for determining valid rail routings and rates — are built point-to-point between these exact BP Locations.

While this works for many industries, it creates fundamental issues for rail-based bulk commodity movements.

The Fundamental Problem: SPLC vs. Business Partner Locations

The rail industry does not use postal addresses for routing or rating. Instead, everything revolves around SPLC codes — standardized railroad station identifiers typically representing city + state combinations or specific rail stations.

Rail carriers publish rates and determine service availability based on these SPLC locations. Rate specialists and traders evaluate moves using questions such as: “Can we move grain from SPLC ‘Des Moines, IA’ to SPLC ‘Dallas, TX’ via Carrier X at published tariff rates?”

This creates a critical disconnect:

  • BP Locations ≠ SPLC Locations: A grain elevator might be located 8–15 miles outside the city whose SPLC the railroad recognizes.
  • No Native Mapping: TM lacks a standard, scalable way to automatically map thousands of BP Locations to the correct SPLC codes.
  • Default Route Dependency: Valid route and rate determination usually requires a point-to-point Default Route between the exact BP Locations. Maintaining routes for thousands of possible combinations is impractical.

Partial Workaround: Zone-Based Default Routes

One commonly attempted approach to address the rigidity of point-to-point Default Routes is to use broad zone-based Default Routes.

In this setup:

  • Origin zones represent large geographic areas (e.g., “Central Iowa Grain Region”).
  • Destination zones represent broad receiving areas (e.g., “Texas Panhandle Processors” or “Pacific Northwest Export Zone”).
  • Intermediate junctions/marshaling yards are defined as key hand-off points.
  • Mainline Class 1 carriers (BNSF, UP, CPKC, etc.) are assigned to execute the long-haul segments between these zones.

This reduces master data volume because one Default Route can cover many possible elevator-to-buyer combinations within the defined zones. The system can then propose mainline routing options and apply published rates more dynamically across broader areas.

However, this approach has a critical limitation:

It cannot effectively model shortline or switch carriers responsible for first-mile pickup (from the actual grain elevator to the mainline interchange) and last-mile delivery (from the mainline drop-off to the final milling company or buyer facility).

Because zone-based routes focus on high-level regional movements and mainline carriers, the critical short-haul legs handled by smaller switch carriers are difficult — if not impossible — to incorporate accurately. These shortline carriers often have their own specific service areas, rates, and access agreements that vary significantly even within the same broad zone. As a result, the zone-based model provides incomplete routing visibility and forces manual handling of first-mile and last-mile segments outside the system.

Business Impact on Commodity Traders

This SPLC vs. BP Location gap, even with zone-based attempts, creates severe operational challenges:

  • Limited Flexibility — Traders cannot systematically validate rail options for new or infrequent origin-destination pairs.
  • High Manual Effort — Rate specialists must research SPLC-based tariffs and shortline options manually.
  • Incomplete Cost Visibility — Especially for full Rule 11 multi-carrier moves involving shortlines.
  • Delayed Execution — Slows down spot trading decisions where logistics cost is a key profitability factor.
  • Scalability Constraints — The problem worsens as trading volumes and supplier/buyer networks expand.

In essence, SAP TM forces a rigid, location-specific structure on an industry that thinks and operates in a more abstract, SPLC-based network model.

Industry Perspective

Traders, rail rate analysts, and transportation planners in the agricultural sector are accustomed to working with SPLC codes and city/state tariff points. They expect a modern TMS to align with this established way of working rather than requiring the industry to adapt its core processes to the system’s structural limitations.

The mismatch between postal-address-based BP Locations and tariff-based SPLC locations remains the single biggest barrier preventing SAP TM from fully supporting dynamic rail transportation of bulk agricultural commodities in North America.

Conclusion

In Part 2, I will focus on how I used the existing TM objects and some enhancements to tackle this challenge providing the Traders with the ability to practically ship from anywhere to anywhere on the North American Railroad network. Stay Tuned!

entry.title: SAP ACC LAC 2026 Wrap-Up: Top Highlights & Direct Links to All Resources
entry.content:
What a wonderful few days we had at the SAP Academic Community Conference Latin America & the Caribbean 2026!

A heartfelt thank you to all professors, academic partners, speakers, SAP colleagues and community members who joined us at SAP Labs Latin America for three days of learning, collaboration, hands-on experiences and meaningful exchange.

It was truly special to welcome such an engaged academic community in São Leopoldo and to explore, together, how SAP learning offerings, innovation, technology and industry collaboration can support the future of teaching and learning across Latin America and the Caribbean. 

Throughout the conference, we had the opportunity to connect with educators and partners from different institutions, exchange experiences, explore SAP academic resources, and discuss how topics such as Business AI, analytics, enterprise systems, simulation-based learning, future talent and digital skills can be brought closer to students in practical and impactful ways. To learn more about the SAP academic community, visit the SAP University Alliances page.

Below, you can find a short recap of each day, as well as the materials shared during the conference.

Day 1 | Welcome, SAP Academic Portfolio & Hands-On Workshops

We started the conference by welcoming participants to SAP Labs Latin America, with opening remarks from Dennison John, Managing Director of the SAP Labs Latin America, Katharina Schäfer, Global Head of Academic Partnerships, and Jesus Aguilar, President of the Academic Board Ibero-America.

The first day focused on introducing the broader SAP academic ecosystem and highlighting learning opportunities available to universities, professors, and students. Participants had the chance to learn more about the SAP University Alliances and Young Thinkers programs and offerings, ERP4students and other opportunities that support teaching and skills development.We counted with the presence of Heather Matthews, Vice President of Academic Partnerships at SAP, Elizabeth Squire, University Competence Center Chico, Sérgio de La Cruz, Business Development Manager at Héc Montréal, and Robert Franz, Program Director at ERP4studens.

In the afternoon, the group joined the SAP Labs Latin America Tour and took part in hands-on workshops and Train-the-Trainer sessions, including experiences with Business Builders – The Hockey Analyst, Research and university-industry collaboration, and Business AI.

Access Day 1 materials here: https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/o2yNEz1?rc=10&doi=SAP1316342&includeRelatedAssets=true

 

Day 2 | Future Talent, Careers, World Café & Interactive Learning

The second day focused on strengthening connections between academia, SAP partners and future talent. Discussions explored how universities and industry partners can work together to better prepare students for careers within the SAP ecosystem, while highlighting the role of academic initiatives in bridging the gap between classroom learning and professional development.

A key highlight was the panel on connecting universities with the SAP partner ecosystem, featuring Glenda Braem, Program Manager, Partner Transformation at SAP; Luciana Cola Santo, Service Partner Manager at SAP; Matheus Gravito, Dual Program, Academic National Manager at Ânima Educação; Marcelo Forti, Head of Operations at Seidor, and Marcelo Salles, CIO at Termomecanica and Fundação Salvador Arena. The session provided valuable perspectives on talent development, industry collaboration, and pathways into the SAP ecosystem.

The day also featured the SAP Employer Branding Campus Strategy session led by Roberta Brunetta, Campus Strategy iXP at SAP. Participants gained insights into SAP’s initiatives designed to prepare future candidates through a combination of technical learning, soft skills development and networking opportunities, offering a comprehensive view of how students can build successful careers within the SAP ecosystem.

Participants also took part in a World Café, an interactive discussion format designed to foster collaboration, knowledge exchange, and collective reflection on key opportunities and challenges facing the academic community.

The activity focused on three strategic themes. Integrating SAP Solutions into Your Curriculum to Engage Gen Z Students explored practical ways to incorporate SAP resources and real-world business scenarios into academic programs to enhance student engagement and learning outcomes. Building Connected Academic Communities focused on strengthening collaboration among educators, institutions, students and SAP to create a more impactful academic network. Finally, Careers & Employment Pathways with SAP Partners examined how universities and industry partners can work together to better prepare students for the workforce, creating clearer pathways to careers within the SAP ecosystem.

The World Café activity provided a valuable platform for participants to share experiences, identify common challenges, and co-create ideas that can help shape the future of education, talent development and industry collaboration.

In the afternoon, the focus shifted to practical learning experiences, with workshops and Train-the-Trainer sessions on SAP Analytics Cloud and ERPsim, allowing participants to explore tools and methodologies that can be applied directly in teaching contexts.

We closed the day with networking and a happy hour, creating another opportunity to continue the conversations and strengthen connections across the community.

Access Day 2 materials here:  https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/k5bxDBL?rc=10&doi=SAP1316344&includeRelatedAssets=true

 

Day 3 | SAP Business AI, SAP Learning Hub & Excellence Award Ceremony

On the final day of the conference, participants explored some of the most important trends shaping the future of education, workforce development, and enterprise technology.

The day began with a session on SAP Business AI, led by Marcelo Palermo, Expert Preventive Solution Support Architect at SAP and Guest Professor in Data Science at Unisinos. Participants explored SAP’s approach to Business AI and how artificial intelligence is being embedded directly into business processes and enterprise applications. The session highlighted how SAP delivers AI that is relevant, reliable and responsible, bringing intelligent capabilities into the everyday work of organizations across areas such as ERP, Human Capital Management, Supply Chain and Customer Experience.

Another highlight was the session Reimagining SAP Learning Experiences through the Tec21 Model for Enterprise AI, presented by Jesus Aguilar, Academy Leader at Tecnológico de Monterrey and member of the SAP Academic Board Ibero-America. The session showcased an innovative executive education model designed to prepare the next generation of SAP consultants with the skills required to thrive in the evolving digital economy of Latin America.

Participants also joined a hands-on session on SAP Learning Hub, student edition, led by Heather Matthews, Vice President of Academic Partnerships at SAP. The session demonstrated how professors and students can access SAP learning content, practical resources, learning journeys, practice systems, and certification opportunities to build in-demand skills and support lifelong learning in an increasingly digital world.

We also revisited the main findings from the World Café activity. This final reflection brought together the ideas, recommendations and insights generated throughout the discussions. It offered a collective reflection on how academia, industry, and SAP can continue working together to advance education, talent development and innovation across the region.

To close the conference, we celebrated outstanding academic collaboration during the Excellence Award Ceremony, recognizing institutions and educators who continue to make a meaningful impact through their engagement with SAP academic initiatives. 

Access Day 3 materials here: https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/hh8nLL4?rc=10&doi=SAP1316345&includeRelatedAssets=true

 

Continuing the journey:

As the SAP Academic Community Conference Latin America & the Caribbean 2026 comes to a close, we would like to express our gratitude to everyone who made this event possible. To our professors, academic leaders, speakers, SAP partners, volunteers, SAP colleagues, and community members, thank you for your active participation and valuable contributions.

Over the course of three days, we shared knowledge, explored emerging technologies, discussed innovative teaching approaches, strengthened relationships across institutions and industries and collectively reflected on how we can better prepare the next generation of talent. The ideas and experiences exchanged throughout the conference demonstrated the strength of this community and its shared commitment to shaping the future of education.

We hope that the conversations started here will continue beyond the conference, inspiring new opportunities. Together, we are building a more connected academic ecosystem.Thank you for being part of the SAP Academic Community LAC 2026. We look forward to continuing this journey together and welcoming you again at future SAP Academic Partnerships initiatives.

Share your feedback:

Your feedback is very important to us. It helps us understand what worked well, what can be improved, and how we can continue creating valuable experiences for the SAP academic community in the region. Share your feedback here:https://sapinsights.eu.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_1Oi8B25WhtYKbz0

Request your Digital Badge:

Participants and speakers can also request their digital badge through the following post. Request your digital badge here: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-university-alliances-program-blog-posts/celebrate-your-sap-academic-community-conference-experience-earn-your/bc-p/14401397#M249

Pictures from the Conference:

You can access some of the best moments from SAP ACC LAC 2026 through the link below. Please use the following password to access the gallery:

Access the pictures here: https://sapext.sharepoint.com/:f:/s/AnonymousLinks/IgCjV5vM4dK2TJpYYp25RFPFATigkFMV3ZRBsBJsdlXShRw

Password: @SAPacclac2026

 

Now we'd love to hear from you.
If you were at the conference, what was the one moment, conversation, or idea that stayed with you?
And looking ahead: what topics or sessions do you feel were missing — and should be on the agenda next time?
Drop your thoughts in the comments below. The conversations that started in São Leopoldo do not have to end here.

 If you have any questions, feel free to post them here, we’d love to continue the conversation with you!

entry.title: Re: Detailed Document Search - Not fetching the goal deta...
entry.content:
@BalajiNudurupat wrote:

Hello Experts,

In our company, we use Detailed Document Search for audit purposes to download the performance review forms for terminated employees.

We tested Latest Experience of Performance Reviews and observed that the admins or HR person with access to Detailed Document Search tool is not able to view the Goal Details of the employee whenever they download the report using Detailed Document Search tool. This was not the case with the legacy experience of Performance Reviews.

We raised a incident with SF and they replied back stating that we need to add F role to the read access of the fields and also on the private-access option on the Goal Plan template. Once we added this, Admins were able to download the report using Detailed Document Search tool but the layout is not as per the legacy layout.

If this is the approach to follow, then we need to add F role to all the prior goal plan templates so that admins can download the reports using Detailed Document Search. But this approach needs more changes to the closed goal plan templates.

I want to know how the other customers are handling this issue with Latest Experience of Performance Review forms.

I raised an influence request to provide the same functionality of Detailed Document Search tool (like prior) with the Latest Experience of Performance Review forms. Appreciate if you can vote for the same.

https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/idea/370907

Thank you.

Hello,


In the Latest Experience of Performance Reviews, many customers face the same limitation with Detailed Document Search not showing goal details by default. The workaround is indeed to add the F role with private‑access on goal plan templates, but that requires updates to older templates and results in a different layout than the legacy view. Most organizations either accept this adjusted layout or raise influence requests, as you did, to push SAP toward restoring the legacy functionality in the new experience.

entry.title: 10.000 filtermember limit in Data Analyzer filter by member section (BW LIVE MODEL)
entry.content:
Hi,

In SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Data Analyzer, when utilizing an SAP BW live connection, the filter member selection list appears to be hard-capped at 10,000 values. We have been unable to find any official Knowledge Base Articles (KBA) or SAP Notes confirming or explaining this specific UI restriction.

Questions:

  • Is this 10,000-value limitation a hard-coded design restriction in the SAC Data Analyzer UI for BW Live connections?

  • Is there a specific SAP Note, BW profile parameter (e.g., in RSADMIN), or SAC configuration that controls this limit and allows us to increase it?

  • If this is standard product behavior, what is the recommended workaround for business users to search and select members that fall outside the initial 10,000 records if there is a workaround?

Note: There are other ways to filter on the valid from date of 2025. I have informed our business users to utilize the filter by range option.

Steps taken:

We encountered this issue when trying to select filter members greater than 18.05.2017 for the InfoObject Valid from (0DATEFROM) (which references 0DATE).

To investigate, we performed the following deep-dive analysis:

  1. Master Data Verification: In the BW Query Builder, the filter value selection correctly displays all values present in the master data table.

  2. Data Export: We exported the entire master data table for 0DATE (sorted ascending) to Excel (via Eclipse). The table contains a total of 30,982 unique values.

    1. SQL QUERY with max rows set to 100000:

      1. SELECT

        /BI0/SDATE~DATE0,

        /BI0/SDATE~SID,

        /BI0/SDATE~CHCKFL,

        /BI0/SDATE~DATAFL,

        /BI0/SDATE~INCFL

        FROM

        /BI0/SDATE

        WHERE

        DATE0 GE '19900101'

        ORDER BY

        DATE0

  3. The 10k Threshold: In Excel, we added a row counter. The date 18.05.2017 is exactly the 10,000th value in the master data table.

    SAC UI Behavior: In the Data Analyzer "Filter by Member" screen, the system displays exactly 501 pagination tabs:

    • The first member is #(Unassigned).

    • Tabs 1 through 500 each hold exactly 20 members (20 times 500 = 10,000).

    • Tab 501 contains only 1 value: 18.05.2017.

    • Result: The UI cuts off completely after exactly 10,000 master data records, meaning the remaining 20,982 members are entirely missing from the selection list.

entry.title: E-Invoices are Changing Everything in Tax Compliance - Here's How to Stay Ahead
entry.content:
Tax authorities around the world are moving fast. E-invoicing mandates are expanding, reporting requirements are tightening, and the bar for real-time compliance is rising. For finance and IT teams, the pressure is mounting - and the old ways of managing invoices and tax compliance simply aren't keeping up.                                                                                                                                         

Here's what's changing, why it's getting harder, and how organizations can turn compliance into a competitive advantage.                 

 

What Is Actually Changing                                                                                                                           

An e-invoice is no longer just a digital version of a paper invoice. It has become a government-enforced exchange and processing framework, with country-specific formats, standards, and submission workflows that must be followed precisely.  Regulatory models can vary significantly by country:                                                                                    

  • Interoperability models (e.g., Belgium) allow exchange for example through Peppol network
  • E-reporting models (e.g., Hungary) require registration of invoice data with authorities after sending invoices to buyers
  • Clearance models (e.g., Mexico) route invoices through tax authorities in real time before they can be considered valid
  • Centralized exchange models (e.g., Italy, Poland) use government-managed platforms to exchange invoices between supplier and buyers
  • 5-corner models (e.g., France, UAE, Oman) introduce additional distribution layers via regulated networks like Peppol network and submission of tax data to authorities within the same flow.   

On top of this, tax authorities are getting smarter with the data they collect. Using, for example, electronic invoices, online cash registers, and SAF-T files, authorities are starting to pre-fill draft VAT returns - and expect taxpayers to either confirm the data is correct or provide evidence to dispute the net liability (real-time audits). As a result, managing 100% consistency between your books of account and the authorities' portals is no longer optional, it is the new baseline.                                                                                                                                 

                 

How SAP Enables This - End to End                                                                                                

SAP's approach is to embed compliance directly into business processes, so it runs in the background without disrupting the way teams work. While compliance complexity increases across jurisdictions, no two countries do e-invoicing exactly the same way. SAP solutions abstract this complexity behind the scenes enabling companies to have one process and one solution across countries and business applications (to learn more about SAP Autonomous Suite, check SAP.com). Instead of disrupting existing workflows, it instead leverages digitalization to take automation to new heights. Let’s see how.                                                                                                                       

Regulated customer invoices

When you create a customer invoice in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, contract accounting and invoicing  and SAP Billing and Revenue Innovation Management (BRIM) or SAP ERP, SAP Document and Reporting Compliance automatically generates the invoice in the required country-specific format and exchanges it with the relevant government portal. A central cockpit gives you visibility into the latest status and flags errors as they occur. And, if you use any other non-SAP billing systems, you can integrate these customer invoices via API and process in the exact same way of SAP native postings (to learn more, you can watch this replay).

With AI-accelerated error resolution, AI helps you understand errors faster - translating technical error messages into plain language, mapping them to SAP terminology, and suggesting corrective actions. What used to take hours of back-and-forth can now be resolved in seconds. To learn more, you can watch this video.                                                                                                                                                               

Regulated supplier invoices

In countries where regulated distribution networks apply - such as government-managed platforms or 5-corner models - SAP Document and Reporting Compliance downloads supplier invoices centrally and routes them to the right business system automatically. Typically, authorities allow to retrieve invoices only by tax number, irrespective of how many systems you might use for processing. For example, you might have intercompany transactions processed directly in SAP S/4HANA, third-party supplier invoices processed leveraging SAP Business Network and SAP Ariba Invoicing, and travel expenses submitted by employees with Concur Expenses. You can now configure rules based on invoice data, so the right invoice always reaches the right business system. Link to SAP Help documentation.

As authorities step into the flow between suppliers and buyers, you can transform the complexity into an opportunity for greater automation.

  • Straight-through invoice processing. Through native integration with SAP Business Network, SAP Ariba Invoicing, and SAP Invoice Management by OpenText, incoming invoices are used to automatically populate SAP fields for invoice posting. Existing invoice validation workflows - including the 3-way match - continue to run unchanged. Invoices that pass validations are auto posted with no manual intervention, from suppliers to postings in the system of record without interventions. Depending on country-specific requirements, upon completion of invoice processing, authorities are automatically notified of approval, rejection or any other status update, making compliance a native step within your Account Payable operations.
  • Travel expenses without the paperwork. For travel expenses, invoices can be routed to Concur Expenses where they are matched to employees automatically.  Expense items appear pre-populated in the employee's home page - including official invoice IDs like Krajowy System e-Faktur (KSeF) reference number in Poland - eliminating the need to upload receipts and populate expense details manually. From manual scanning of paper receipts to expenses available to be submitted in a claim.

Once invoices are posted, the data feeds into e-reporting and payment processes, including notifying authorities of payment status or embedding official invoice numbers into payment files.

Continuous consistency and submissions

By integrating e‑invoicing into core processes, you can reduce compliance risk and eliminate end‑of‑period crunch. This means moving beyond period-end submissions toward:                                                                                  

  • Real-time consistency checks that compare the business data in your system of record against what tax authorities have on record, resolving discrepancies before they escalate
  • Continuous submissions via electronic documents or reports - aligning authority portals with ERP data throughout the month and giving teams more time to act.                                                                                                                   

The result: audit-ready, any day of the month. Rather than a period-end scramble, compliance activity is spread evenly across the month. When discrepancies appear - whether missing documents or data mismatches - they are flagged, analyzed, and resolved quickly. At the next comparison run, resolved items update automatically. No outstanding differences means nothing to worry about.

To learn more, you can watch this video.                                                                                          

Period-end statutory reporting and tax payments

At period-end, SAP Document and Reporting Compliance keeps your statutory reporting on track: automatically preparing returns in the legally required format, while a central dashboard gives you visibility into deadlines across all entities and countries. Automated deadline tracking and submission reduce the risk of non-compliance and remove manual handoffs.                                                                    

  • Traditional returns prepared from ERP data give tax experts full control: review data, make traceable adjustments fully integrated with accounting records, and drill down from any return line to the underlying business documents. The Manage Tax Items app lets you flexibly slice and dice data, identify documents that need correction, and act on them directly - with audit-proof traceability and no offline documentation required. To learn more, you can watch this video.
  • Draft returns pre-populated by tax authorities can be retrieved directly, reconciled against your ERP data, corrected where needed, and digitally signed - all within the same solution. To learn more, you can watch this video.             

When your reports are ready, a configurable up to two-step approval workflow ensures the right sign offs are in place before submission. For high-volume scenarios, returns can be prepared automatically as deadlines approach and submitted in the background once the review step is complete - keeping your team focused on exceptions, not administration.                    

 

The Bottom Line                                                                                                                           

E-invoicing is not a temporary regulatory hurdle. It is the foundation of a broader shift toward real-time, data-driven tax compliance. Tax authorities are collecting more data, pre-filling tax returns, and closing the gap between what businesses report and what authorities already know.                                                                                                                              

Organizations that treat this as a process transformation, rather than a compliance checkbox, will be better positioned to reduce risk, cut operational effort, and stay ahead of what comes next.

The question is not whether to adapt. It is how fast you can make compliance invisible. More to come on how agentic AI is making Autonomous Compliance a reality - stay tuned.

entry.title: SAP NetWeaver network requirements for GUI, Print Service, KPro, and Crystal Reports
entry.content:
I’m currently collecting technical information related to network communication for SAP NetWeaver and several related components, including:

  • SAP GUI

  • Print Service

  • KPro

  • Crystal Reports

To ensure these components can communicate reliably in a secured environment, I’m looking for confirmed details about their external connectivity requirements, specifically outbound communication from our internal network to the Internet.

Could anyone provide the following information for the components listed above?

  • Required ports — the TCP and/or UDP ports required for outbound communication from our internal network to the Internet (e.g., for license activation or cloud services)

  • Protocols used — the network protocols utilized

  • System name — the exact name of the external system or service that our internal systems must connect to

  • Target IP addresses — the external IP addresses or hostnames that our internal systems must be able to reach

Any official documentation references or firsthand configuration experience would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Re: Crystal Reports PDF bookmark displays incorrect Japan...
entry.content:
Thank you for the additional guidance.

We performed further checks in the current environment.

Current findings:
- The report body text renders correctly.
- The issue occurs only in the PDF bookmarks / outline.
- In Adobe Acrobat Reader (Menu > Document Properties > Fonts), the PDF shows:
- MeiryoUI (Embedded Subset), Type: TrueType
- a second embedded subset TrueType font entry whose font name appears blank
- Meiryo UI is present in Windows fonts on the DEV PC.
- The batch-side report generation projects target .NET Framework 4.7.2.

One important constraint on our side is that changing the font family is not realistic, because the font is effectively fixed by customer requirements.
Therefore, we would like to ask specifically for a solution or workaround under the condition that MeiryoUI must continue to be used.

Could you please advise:
1) whether PDF bookmarks / outline in Crystal Reports are known to use a different font/resource path than the report body,
2) whether there is any known workaround to make bookmarks use MeiryoUI consistently,
3) whether there are any export settings, report settings, or runtime-side options that can prevent bookmark-only garbled Japanese text while continuing to use MeiryoUI.

If runtime update is still considered necessary, we will separately review whether that is feasible in our environment.
However, if there is any workaround that can be applied without changing the font family, that would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: SAP Public Cloud: advanced intercompany sales (5D2) - currency is different in IC SO and SO
entry.content:
In standard, the Sales document currency is always fetched from the customer master. In an intercompany sales scenario, this may lead to different currencies in both orders which may lead to rounding differences. 

Sales Org: France (EUR),

Customer in UK (GBP),

Delivering Plant Netherlands (EUR)

--> Customer facing sales order will show GBP and IC SO will show EUR (because Sales Org France will be the customer of IC SO)

 

Is it possible to influence the document currency of this IC SO to have consistent currencies in this whole flow?

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 02 - Content for common reuse types
Re: How to configure business goals that can change in time
entry.content: For each plan, the section weight needs to be 60% for business goals and then 40% for individual goals.   If you need to convert the goal weights from adding to 60% to adding to 100%, multiply the existing weight by 5 and then divide by 3.   For example, 30% would multiply by 5 to make it 150% and then divide by 3 to give you the proper result of 50%.

entry.title: Manual Value Cases: Track Transformation Value From Any Source
entry.content:
What Manual Value Cases Deliver

Flexibility in value capture: Transformation Leads can now create value cases directly within an Initiative. Define the metric, set baseline and target values, specify the time period—all within the familiar Transformation Management experience.

Consistent measurement: Manual Value Cases use the same metric types as PAM-sourced value cases:

  • Monetary values (cost savings, revenue impact)
  • Non-monetary values (efficiency gains, quality improvements)
  • Duration units (days, weeks, months, hours, minutes, seconds)

Unified visibility: All value cases—whether sourced from PAM or entered manually—appear together in the Value Cases table. Each value case clearly indicates its source, maintaining transparency for reporting.

Data integrity: Manual Value Cases are separated from PAM-sourced value cases at the data level. This preserves the integrity of automated value tracking while extending your ability to capture the full transformation story.

Designed for Transformation Leads

The workflow is straightforward:

  1. Navigate to any Initiative
  2. Open the Value Cases table
  3. Create a new manual value case
  4. Define your metric type, baseline, and target values
  5. Set the relevant time period

The side panel guides you through each step. Edit or delete value cases as your transformation evolves. 

 

 

For detailed guidance, visit the SAP Help Portal .

Coming soon:

  • Currency conversion — Handle monetary values across currencies, consistent with how PAM value cases work today
  • Faster value case creation — Streamlined workflows to connect value cases to your process landscape
  • Improved discoverability — Better guidance for teams getting started with value tracking

entry.title: Re: Requirement for Project-wise Customer and Supplier Li...
entry.content:
Dear @sivakumar_k571 

Did you try already the following blog post ?
Custom fields for the "Manage Customer Line Items"... - SAP Community

This blog post describes an example how to use a custom field called "Responsible Unit" to manage customer line items. To fill the custom field, the extension point Journal Entry Substitution (FIN_ACDOC_SUBSTITUTION) is used.

You could map this example to the described requirement. Prerequisite to do so is, that you can define a custom logic  which can derive the project information based on the data of the Journal Entry. The extension point Journal Entry Substitution (FIN_ACDOC_SUBSTITUTION) provides as input the the Journal Entry header as well as all Journal Entry Line Items. That is you can for example also analyze the revenue line items to derive a value for the custom field in the customer line item.

Best regards
Steffen Riemann

entry.title: Re: Connection between SAC Analytics Cloud and SAP HANA C...
entry.content:
@Stephany429  Yes, this connection is possible. It mainly depends on whether you want to use a live connection or create an imported model in SAC.

For SAP HANA Cloud on BTP, SAC supports a native live connection. In this case, the data stays in SAP HANA Cloud and SAC consumes it directly. For this setup, you do not need the SAP Analytics Cloud Agent or the SAP Cloud Connector.

SAP documentation:
Live Data Connection to SAP HANA Cloud

If you are trying to create a standard SAC model by importing data from SAP HANA, then the setup is different. For an import connection to SAP HANA, SAP requires the SAP Cloud Connector, SAP Analytics Cloud Agent, and SAP Java Connector.

SAP documentation:
Import Data Connection to SAP HANA

However, if your model is created in SAP Datasphere, for example as an Analytic Model, then I would recommend connecting SAC directly to Datasphere using a live connection, instead of connecting SAC to the underlying SAP HANA Cloud database.

SAP documentation:
Live Data Connections to SAP Datasphere

So, for our scenario, if the data model is already available in Datasphere or HANA Cloud and we only want to consume it for reporting in SAC, the live connection would be the simpler and cleaner option. The SAC Agent and Cloud Connector would only be relevant if we specifically need an import-based connection.

entry.title: Joule Studio at SAP Sapphire 2026: Ten Days, Two Cities, One Clear Signal
entry.content:
As a Product Manager for SAP Build, focused on Early Adopter and Product Enablement, SAP Sapphire is one of the most important moments in the year - not just to present what we have built, but to hear directly from the people building with it. This year, the SAP Build PM team was deeply embedded across both events: in the ASUG pre-conference, the keynotes, the sessions, and the Agent Lab. We asked our PM colleagues to do what product managers do best - observe closely, listen carefully, and capture what they saw. This post is the result of that: a collective picture of what Joule Studio's debut at Sapphire looked and felt like, from the people who were there.

SAP Sapphire 2026 was held across two cities - Orlando (May 11–13) and Madrid (May 19–21) - and the energy in both was unmistakable. Joule Studio made its public debut at Sapphire. Thousands of customers, partners, and developers experienced it hands-on: some for the first time, some building on what they had already seen in Florida. The conversation that started in the US continued in Europe with the same momentum and the same conclusion. Enterprise AI has stopped being a promise and started being something you can build in an afternoon.

 

Orlando: Learning by Building

SAP Sapphire Orlando set the tone for what this year's event would be about. Christian Klein opened the keynote with a question that reframes everything: will SAP even be a software company in the future? His answer - that SAP is becoming a business AI company - was not defensive. It was clarifying.

The core argument landed clearly: AI keeps stalling in enterprises not because the models are lacking, but because they are trained on the public internet, not on your business. They have no knowledge of your processes, your data, or your compliance framework. SAP Business AI Platform - and Joule Studio within it - is built around fixing exactly that.

Christian Klein introducing SAP Business AI Platform during the Keynote

Joule Studio was introduced as an intent-based, deeply contextualized build environment for agents, open to any third-party model with no lock-in and both low-code and pro-code paths - with agent development free and backed by a €100M+ ecosystem investment. Throughout the event, there was a noticeable shift away from isolated AI use cases toward AI-infused end-to-end experiences. Attendees were not looking for another AI experiment - they wanted proof that this works in production. Real-world customer journeys, with Fujitsu cited as a concrete reference multiple times, drove that conversation.

Muhammad Alam introducing SAP Autonomous Suite during the Keynote

The keynote by Dr. Michael Ameling, Jonathan von Rüden, Jan Oberhauser, André Christ, and Maria Kondratyeva featured a live Joule Studio demonstration that became the defining moment of the event. The team demonstrated a 3-way invoice matching agent built end-to-end on stage. The scenario was immediately relatable: a company processing 50,000 invoices monthly, chronic matching exceptions, and approvals consistently landing with the wrong people.

Joule Studio received a natural language description of the problem. What followed was automated and complete: SAP LeanIX integration discovering the relevant SAP systems, SAP Signavio applying 50 years of SAP process expertise to understand matching strategies and routing rules, generated orchestration workflows for exception handling and escalation, and Agent Hub providing governance from the first deployment.

The result: Accounts Payable (AP) exception cycle time reduced from 72 hours to 90 seconds. Five manual touchpoints eliminated to zero.

One audience reaction said it all: “I couldn't tell if it was a real system or a guided demo.”  That comment - part skepticism, part disbelief - repeated throughout the week. The bar for proving production-readiness is high. Joule Studio cleared it.

Christian Klein introducing the Autonomous Enterprise during the Keynote

 

The Agent Lab: From Skepticism to Conviction

If the keynote introduced Joule Studio, the Agent Lab made it personal. Over three days, more than 1,000 unique customers and partners queued up and nobody left early. The numbers are striking: 3,366 AI solutions created, 1,640 agents built, across Finance, HR, Supply Chain, and beyond.

The Agent Lab in Orlando

The experience followed a consistent pattern: skepticism at the start, hands-on building in the middle, and genuine surprise at the end. The barrier was never ideas - it was trust that the platform would actually work. Once they saw their own business problem translated into a working design, that trust arrived instantly.

As Samir Hamichi from the Agent Lab team observed: “People were looking to know the added value from SAP, what [was] the differentiator. When they walked through the experience, they understood the power of tools and strategy.”

The Agent Lab in Orlando

 

What Makes Joule Studio Different

Every AI coding tool on the market can write code. What they cannot do is understand your business. Generic tools operate at the file and repository level - they have no knowledge of your procure-to-pay process, your enterprise architecture, or the SAP capabilities already available to you. Context is lost at every handoff.

Joule Studio changes the starting point entirely. The SAP Knowledge Graph, process models from SAP Signavio, enterprise architecture from SAP LeanIX, and SAP Domain Models all flow automatically into every development artifact from the first session. A business analyst describes a problem in plain language. Joule Studio generates a structured, SAP-context-aware Product Requirements Document - a shared source of truth for business stakeholders, developers, and architects, aligned from the first output rather than after weeks of back-and-forth. That PRD becomes a Technical Specification; the specification becomes production-grade, clean-core-aligned code, deployable to a fully managed runtime.

The insight heard repeatedly across the event from customers: “This brings business back into the loop.” And from one partner architect: “Context at scale - Signavio, LeanIX. That is powerful.”

One participant shared on social media what struck with him most: the end-to-end completeness. From describing the intent to generating a PRD, functional and technical specifications, unit tests, and a deployable solution - all without leaving the platform. What surprised him most was that the platform itself guides you through the process, asking clarifying questions like a colleague would rather than expecting you to have all the answers upfront. As he put it, you are not just building agents - you are building them with the help of agents.

You are not building an agent that connects to your business afterward. You are building one that is grounded in it from the first sentence you type.

SAP Business AI Platform

 

The Governance Conversation Nobody Skips

One exchange at the Orlando Agent Lab deserves to be named directly. An attendee, after seeing what agents could do, said: “Now I can cut half my company.”

Maria Kondratyeva pushed back immediately: “That is not what agents are for. Agents eliminate soul-crushing manual work - data entry, chasing approvals, reconciling exceptions - so people can focus on work that requires judgment, creativity, and relationship-building. The work only humans can do.”

But the fact that the question was asked reflects how seriously people are taking this. The governance features in Joule Studio - Agent Hub, LeanIX integration, and human-in-the-loop approval gates - are not compliance checkboxes. They are what separates enterprise AI from unmanageable risk. The governance story needs to be as prominent as the capability story - and across both cities, the sessions that included it landed better every time.

 

Madrid: The Momentum Crosses the Atlantic

One week later, Madrid picked up exactly where Orlando left off. Dr. Michael Ameling, Jonathan von Rüden, Jan Oberhauser, André Christ, and Soufia Maherzi brought the same invoice matching demonstration to a European audience and got the same reaction. A fresh crowd, the same disbelief turning into belief.

The Madrid Agent Lab wrapped up with 765 customers and partners, 1,350 AI solutions created, and 770 agents built. Different city, same energy: long queues, nobody leaving, and that consistent moment of surprise when an attendee's business problem became a fully designed agent before their eyes.

As Friederike Marby recalled: “Agent Lab was intense in the best possible way. The sheer volume of feedback, the questions, the ideas flying around. We had customers who flat-out didn't believe it was real, almost looking for reasons to dismiss it. But what really stayed with me was watching them discover, on their own terms, that it absolutely was.”

 The Madrid Agent Lab Team

 

Final Thoughts

What struck us most, putting this post together, was the consistency of what our colleagues reported across both cities - independent observations that told the same story. The skepticism at the keynote. The energy in the Agent Lab. The moment attendees stopped asking “can this really work?” and started asking “what do I build first?”

That shift - from doubt to conviction, not through a polished pitch but through direct, hands-on experience - is exactly what we set out to enable. Joule Studio's debut at Sapphire was not just a product launch. It was the beginning of a much larger conversation between SAP and the enterprise community about what it means to build with AI in a way that is grounded, governed, and genuinely useful.

We are grateful to our PM colleagues whose impressions and observations brought this post to life - and to every customer, partner, and developer who showed up, asked hard questions, and built something.

 

What is Next

Across two cities and six days of hands-on building, the combined numbers tell their own story: thousands of agents created, thousands of attendees who arrived skeptical and left with a deployment plan.

Joule Studio is planned to reach General Availability in Q3 2026. The trial is free, and the starting point is a business problem you already know you have.

 

Want to watch the sessions?

- Listen to the keynote - The Beginning of Better

- Discover the Demo - The Business AI Platform for your future

- Watch the Deep Dive - From intent to execution: Build AI agents with Joule Studio

- Hear the Strategy - The enterprise path to agentic AI

All Sapphire recordings are available here

Register for upcoming webinars here

 

Ready to start building?

Visit Joule Studio to learn more and register your interest.

Explore the Autonomous Enterprise Learning Journey for guided tutorials and deeper dives into specific capabilities.

The enterprise AI era is not approaching. In Orlando and Madrid, SAP customers and partners are already building it, one agent at a time.

entry.title: Re: Upgrade SAP Cloud Connector to 2.19.0

Hi,

we have the same problem and SAP answered on this:

At present, version 2.19.0 has not yet been released for use in ECS. The responsible ECS team is still validating the version as part of the internal release management process. Therefore, an upgrade to this version is currently not possible.

Once the version is approved and released by ECS, it will become available in the upgrade options accordingly.

Your scheduling happens at the Maintenance plan level, not in the item maintenance item level. This is not possible with this setup. 
You need to create separate maintenance plans and schedule based on the different dates.

entry.title: Secondary Cost G/Ls Not Visible in Project Costs App
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We are using SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and have observed that in the Project Costs app, only values posted to Primary Cost and Revenue G/L accounts are displayed. Postings to Secondary Cost G/L accounts are not shown, although they are visible in the Display Line Items app.

Our understanding is that the Project Costs app considers only the G/L accounts assigned in YPS2, and since only Primary Cost and Revenue G/Ls are maintained there, Secondary Cost G/L postings are excluded.

Could you please confirm:

  1. Is this standard system behavior?
  2. Is it correct that YPS2 should contain only Primary Cost and Revenue G/L accounts?
  3. Should Secondary Cost G/Ls also be assigned in YPS2 if they need to be reported in the Project Costs app, or is that not the recommended approach?

Thank you for your guidance.

Rohini Dutta

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 02 - Content for common reuse types
Positioning SAP Business Data Cloud for the Retail industry
entry.content:
Retail leaders are being asked to do more with less. More personalization. More resilience. More visibility. Yet only 34% of business leaders fully trust their data.

That gap creates a huge opportunity for SAP Partners.

In our upcoming webinar, Positioning SAP Business Data Cloud for the Retail Industry, we'll go beyond product messaging and explore how SAP Business Data Cloud fits into a modern retail architecture.

We'll cover how SAP BDC brings together SAP and non-SAP data, supports predictive replenishment and demand forecasting, enables the data product economy, and lays the foundation for autonomous retail powered by Agentic AI.

Plus, you'll see a live demo and learn how to connect these capabilities to executive priorities across merchandising, pricing, inventory, and supply chain operations.

Whether you implement solutions, build extensions, or lead customer conversations, this session will help you position SAP BDC with greater confidence and credibility.

Register now and join us.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/may/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00014218.html

entry.title: how to custom agent for sales price approval
entry.content:
Hi sap community,

Regarding the sales price approval function, I am currently testing two scenarios:

1. Scenario 1: Custom Team Type with Custom Responsibility Definition I created a custom team type with one standard responsibility definition and one custom responsibility definition named "Price Channel" (which contains two sample values, A and B). I then set up two separate teams—one assigned to custom responsibility definition value A and the other to value B—in order to split the approval routing between the teams.

  • The Issue: However, during testing, the system still sends approval notifications to all users assigned as "Sales Price Approver – Level 1" across both teams, according to the settings in the Manage Sales Price Workflows app.

2. Scenario 2: Creator-based Approver Determination (If Creator is A, Approver is B) I am testing a case where if the document creator is User A, the designated approver must be User B. To achieve this, I have configured the responsibility rule, context, and custom responsibility rule logic (BAdI/Custom Logic).

  • The Issue: Similar to Scenario 1, the system completely ignores the filters and continues to blast approval notifications to all users belonging to "Sales Price Approver – Level 1" in both teams based on the workflow configuration.

Could you please help point out what might be causing this root issue, and how I can achieve the expected behavior for both scenarios?

Thank you in advance for your support!

entry.title: BTP IntegrationSuite SalesfrocePubSub adapter limits/costs
entry.content:
We are currently implementing integrations between Salesforce and SAP ECC using SAP Integration Suite / CPI and the Salesforce Pub/Sub adapter.

Our current design is event-driven, where Salesforce outbound interfaces publish events to Salesforce Pub/Sub queues/topics, which are then consumed by CPI and forwarded synchronously to SAP ECC through XI/proxy interfaces.

At the moment, the expected landscape would include approximately 20 different Salesforce event queues/topics, which would likely translate into around 20 Salesforce Pub/Sub adapter consumer instances/connections running in CPI.

We would like to better understand the potential operational and performance implications of this architecture on the SAP BTP Integration Suite tenant.

Specifically, we would appreciate guidance regarding:

  • Expected impact of multiple concurrent Pub/Sub adapter subscriptions on CPI runtime resources
    Memory/thread/worker consumption considerations for persistent gRPC streaming connections
  • Recommended sizing or scalability limits for Salesforce Pub/Sub consumers
  • Any known limitations, recommendations, or customer experiences regarding large-scale Salesforce Pub/Sub adapter usage

entry.title: Inquire about PO's with milestones (%) from PR's with totals (values)
entry.content:
Hello,

I would like to ask you about the following business scenario that we have and if you could tellme if there is a Standard solution for it.

From a Purchase Requisition with X positions in which each positions is a different Item with a value, we need to convert it into a PO with the same positions. But it is needed to reflect in the header the many number of milestones (percentage for payment that add up to 100%) that represent the invoices that will arrive from the supplier in different dates.

It is needed because the integration with VIM needs those percentages values to be able to automatically post the invoices when they arrive as they will not have the value of the items nor the GR, it will have the percentage value.

¿Is there a standard way of doing this that could integrate with MM-FI-VIM?

Example:

PR:

4 positions - 1 for 50€ / 1 for 300€ / 1 for 500€ / 1 for 150€ (Total 1000€)

PO:

4 positions - 1 for 50€ / 1 for 300€ / 1 for 500€ / 1 for 150€ (Total 1000€)

3 milestones - 35% 350€/ 40% 400€/

entry.title: Preventing Negative Stock Postings in WM Interim Storage Types
entry.content:
Hi Team,

We are facing stock inconsistencies in WM due to pending WM activities (TR/PCN to TO conversion and confirmation), which eventually result in negative stock situations in interim storage types.

To prevent this, I tried disabling the "Allow Negative Stocks" indicator in the storage type configuration. However, the system still allows negative stock postings in the interim storage type when goods issue is posted.

Could you please advise on the following:

1. Is there any standard SAP configuration to completely prevent negative stock postings in WM interim storage types?
2. Why does the system continue to post negative stock even after disabling the Allow Negative Stocks setting for the interim storage type?
3. Are there any additional WM controls, exits, BADIs, or validations available to block such postings?
4. What is the SAP-recommended approach to ensure IM and WM stock remain synchronized and avoid stock inconsistencies caused by pending WM activities?

Any relevant SAP Notes, best practices, or recommendations would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you,
Leelamohan

entry.title: PartnerEdge Build: SAP Transportation Management (TM) Not Appearing in Price List
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are an ISV registered under the SAP PartnerEdge Build program and are working to build a certified integration between our platform and SAP Transportation Management (SAP TM) embedded in S/4HANA.

Our product is a freight market intelligence and data platform — we deliver real-time spot rates, capacity indices, and carrier benchmarking data via API. The use case for SAP TM users is surfacing this market data natively within transportation planning and carrier selection workflows.

The problem: We cannot find the SAP TM partner test/dev environment anywhere in our PartnerEdge price list.

Here is the navigation path we have exhausted:
• Categories → Cloud - S/4HANA Applications → Line of Business Portfolio → Cloud - S/4HANA Digital Supply Chain
• The only three products listed there are SAP Project and Resource Management (8021070), SAP Service and Asset Manager (8005710), and SAP Intelligent Product Recommendation (8016066) — none of which are relevant to Transportation Management.

We have also checked:
• Cloud - S/4HANA Digital Supply Chain (top-level shortcut) — same three products
• Cloud - S/4HANA Digital Enterprise Platform
• Partner Test, Demo, and Development On-Premise

We have already contacted the SAP Partner Portal support team, who were unable to resolve this and redirected us here.

Our specific questions:
1. What is the correct product SKU / license for an SAP PartnerEdge Build partner to access an SAP TM development and test environment on S/4HANA Cloud?
2. Does SAP TM require a separate entitlement request outside the standard price list — i.e., does it need to be manually enabled by SAP's partner team?
3. Is SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform) a prerequisite that needs to be provisioned first before TM becomes visible?
4. Has anyone else run into this and found a resolution path?

Any guidance from partners who have successfully navigated this, or from SAP product/partner team members, would be greatly appreciated. Happy to share more detail about our integration use case if helpful.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Split Billing Documents Posting Into Multiple Account...
entry.content:
Go to SPRO > Sales and Distribution > Billing > Billing Documents > Country/Region-Specific Features > Maintain Maximum Number Of Billing Items. Here, you have the options to restrict the number of line items in billing.

In ECC 617, there is no standard FI-side split functionality like in S/4HANA. The only way to meet the requirement is through custom development either by enhancing FI posting logic (via BAdIs/exits) or by redesigning aggregation rules to reduce line items.

entry.title: Re: Preventing Negative Stock Postings in WM Interim Stor...
entry.content:
Hello @Leelamohan 

I think you have made the correct configuration. But there is one more piece to the puzzle. Check the documentation of the "Allow negative stock" field:

You also need to customize L9040 message status - SPRO: Interfaces -> Inventory Management -> Allow Negative Stocks in Interim Storage Types -> Control of System Messages in Warehouse Management.

The standard setting is blank, meaning no message. Change it to E and retest.

Technical explanation

Procedure CHECK_NEGATIVE_STOCK_ALLOWED, include LLCMBF1D verifies if negative stock is allowed:

      CALL FUNCTION 'ME_CHECK_T160M'
           EXPORTING
                I_ARBGB = 'L9'
                I_MSGNR = '040'
                I_MSGVS = PARAM_MSV
           EXCEPTIONS
                ERROR   = 1
                NOTHING = 2
                WARNING = 3
                OTHERS  = 4.

      move sy-subrc to sav_subrc.                            "v_n_740871

      write: p_menge to lv_menge.
      concatenate lv_menge p_meins into lv_msgv2
                  separated by space.

      CASE sav_SUBRC.
        WHEN 1.
*         MESSAGE E040 WITH P_MATNR P_MENGE P_MEINS P_LGTYP.   "n_670743
          MESSAGE E040 WITH P_MATNR lv_msgv2 P_LGTYP P_LGPLA.
        WHEN 3.
*         MESSAGE W040 WITH P_MATNR P_MENGE P_MEINS P_LGTYP.   "n_670743
          MESSAGE W040 WITH P_MATNR lv_msgv2 P_LGTYP P_LGPLA.
                                                             "^_n_740871
      ENDCASE.

The message is ignored if its status is not E or W.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: I am unable to delete BP relationships 33099736 and 38085025 due to active transactions.
entry.content: The message indicates that the Business Partner (BP) relationships 33099736 and 38085025 have active transactions and cannot be deleted. This is standard SAP behavior: BP relationships involved in active transactions or documents cannot be deleted until those transactions are closed, completed, or archived. The issue occurs when trying to delete these relationships, likely in SAP for Me or related systems.

entry.title: E-Reporting Vs E-Invoicing for External Billing Documents
entry.content:
Hi,

We are trying to establish a link between E-Invoicing versus E-Reporting for Invoices which are coming to SAP DRC from non-SAP system. Non-SAP systems feeds the invoice data to SAP DRC in UBL 2.1 format and then SAP DRC creates its e-Document .

In the BP we maintain country key=FR and FR2 (SIREN) with taxstatus=PRA hence making the customer as established entity in France. so it qualifies for the Domestic B2B transaction and hence the E-Invoicing.

In some customers we maintain country key=FR and FR2 (SIREN) with taxstatus=NOR hence making the customer as Non-established entity in France. so it qualifies for the Domestic B2C transaction and hence E-Reporting.

For SAP native invoices i-e Billing document generated via SD or FI postings, above BP logic is working fine and it is routing the invoice data correctly to E-Invoicing or E-Reporting folder.

For the invoices coming from non-SAP system as UBL invoice, necessary business transformation is happening in the middleware and then it is replicated to SAP DRC and SAP DRC is creating e-Invoice for every case disregarding the FR2 TAXSTATUS=PRA OR NOR.  I am not sure why SAP standard logic does not work  for these invoices coming from external systems. Has anyone encountered this issue ? Will SAP standard logic not work for non-SAP invoices coming in UBL 2.1 format and we will have to do this transformation in middleware ?

Any experiences will be appreciated.

Thanks

Atif

entry.title: Re: What is clean core anyway?
entry.content:
@Denver_Fletcher91  - Great point...  A clean core (all five principles) is essential to run an effective autonomous enterprise. Business context and data are foundational for utilizing agents effectively.

  • Using standard processes (best practices) provides the ability to utilize out-of-the-box AI that already has a great business context.
  •  It emphasizes reliable data and semantics.  AI quality depends on consistent master/transactional data and end‑to‑end telemetry.
  • Governing extensibility and integration are important so the agents interact via published APIs/events on SAP BTP, avoiding brittle exits. 
  • Clean core provides a stable upgrade path so agents keep working when your core is updated, because they’re not tied to in‑core custom code. 
  • Using Agent Hub will also provide a governance framework for using agents in the context of business processes (And hopefully avoid sprawl of agents 😊)
